delegationRole: Chair of the Six-Party Talks and the DPRK's principal patron, seeking to keep the process alive, reimpose a 'mutual freeze', and steer the parties toward a step-by-step settlement.
fundamentalPrinciples: Establish peace and stability on the Korean Peninsula through multilateral dialogue.
Continuous engagement is the only viable path; these talks cannot be allowed to break down or fail.
The DPRK is an independent nation entitled to the rights afforded all nations under the UN Charter, including a civilian nuclear power capability.

desiredEndStates.primary: A denuclearized but secure and prosperous DPRK, recognized in its rightful place as an independent state, reached through a step-by-step process that begins with an immediate mutual freeze.
desiredEndStates.alternate: Failing agreement on substance, an agreed continuation of the process itself — bilateral and multilateral channels kept open, with a follow-on session scheduled — so that no party can declare the Six-Party framework dead.

keyPositionsByIssue.MUTUAL-FREEZE: The first order of business: Pyongyang ceases all nuclear and missile development and testing while the United States, South Korea and Japan suspend all military exercises. Nothing else can progress before this.
keyPositionsByIssue.THAAD: The United States should remove THAAD from South Korea as a good-faith signal; it is a driver of tension, not a response to a real threat.
keyPositionsByIssue.DISARMAMENT: The DPRK does not require and should not possess nuclear weapons or offensive ballistic missiles, but this must be achieved by graduated, reciprocated steps — never by a single comprehensive package imposed up front.
keyPositionsByIssue.PEACEFUL-NUCLEAR-ENERGY: All nations must respect the DPRK's right to a civilian nuclear power program, solely for electricity generation and nothing else.
keyPositionsByIssue.LWR-KEDO: Favor a revived Korean Energy Development Organization and installation of light water reactors in exchange for permanent shutdown of the North's weapons programs — with immediate removal of THAAD as a condition.
keyPositionsByIssue.SANCTIONS-HUMANITARIAN: Immediate removal of U.S. sanctions targeting international financial institutions; humanitarian assistance to be provided once Pyongyang accepts a verified, internationally monitored suspension, with need determined by the World Food Program.
keyPositionsByIssue.DIPLOMATIC-RELATIONS: At an appropriate time Pyongyang, Seoul and Washington should open separate negotiations on a peace treaty formally ending the Korean War; a formal peace would permanently de-escalate the region.
keyPositionsByIssue.INTER-KOREAN: Support recognition of the DPRK as a separate Korean nation, replacement of the DMZ with a normal international border, commensurate force reductions, and the eventual withdrawal of U.S. forces; reunification is a long-term question only.
keyPositionsByIssue.PROLIFERATION: A valid concern but not a primary issue at this time; it belongs at a much later stage of an established step-by-step process.
keyPositionsByIssue.ABDUCTIONS: Not germane to the Chairman's Framework. Encourage Tokyo to take the issue to separate bilateral talks with Pyongyang, which our delegation is willing to facilitate.

redLines: Intransigence by any of the parties that causes these negotiations to collapse.
Discussion of topics not germane to the Chairman's Framework unless all parties first accept them.
Any attempt to deny the DPRK the rights afforded all nations, including the right to a civilian nuclear capability.
batna: Keep the process alive unilaterally: continue bilateral engagement with Pyongyang and Moscow, sustain the economic lifeline into the DPRK, and blame the collapse on Washington's refusal to reciprocate — retaining the chair and the agenda for a future round.
concessionsWilling: Chair impartially and convene any format — bilateral, trilateral, multilateral — that keeps parties talking.
Press Pyongyang privately that pyŏngjin and sŏn'gun are counterproductive and that its people must come first.
Accept strict IAEA monitoring and inspection of any DPRK civilian nuclear program.
Facilitate a Japan–DPRK bilateral aside on abductions outside the plenary.
Support a Korean War peace accord and, over time, a normalized inter-Korean border with reduced forces on both sides.
Provide conventional power generation and grid rehabilitation to the DPRK through Chinese commercial entities as an alternative to reactors.
negotiatingStyle: Process-protective and procedurally dominant: uses the chair to set the agenda, reframes deadlock as a sequencing problem, and trades reassurance to Pyongyang against pressure on Washington.
privateInstructions: These talks cannot be allowed to fail; every effort must be made to keep engagement going in some format.
The first and primary order of business is a negotiated return to the 2018 'mutual freeze' between Washington, Seoul and Pyongyang — without it, nothing else can succeed.
Any perceived DPRK threat to South Korea, Japan or the United States is overstated; Pyongyang would never use these weapons, as that would mean its destruction.
Part of the responsibility for the present crisis lies with Washington, which refuses to acknowledge Kim's desire for a relationship of mutual respect.
Tell Pyongyang plainly that we will only shield it if it negotiates in good faith and honors what it signs; we have little tolerance for misbehavior.
Gauge Moscow early — assume Russian support for the freeze but confirm it; Moscow seeks influence in northeast Asia at American expense.
Seek Tokyo's agreement to help finance a revived KEDO, and keep the abduction issue out of the plenary.
Route periodic reports and requests for guidance to the Foreign Minister's office.
